figures.caption: FIG. 1. Snapshots of velocity fields across the Reynolds numbers tested. Colorbar scales given in m/s.
FIG. 2. Schematic of the imaging region of interest, outlined by the black dashed line, relative to the water tunnel and cylinder.
FIG. 3. (a) Cumulative energy of POD modes for Re = 590. (b) Corresponding velocity field reconstruction with varying number of modes.

TABLE I. Average model evaluation time on an NVIDIA A100. All models evaluate faster than the minimum inter-sample time (5 ms). Note that deep learning models may be evaluated even faster if compiled.
FIG. 4. Actual and predicted y-component velocity fields with error for all models. The results of the (a) first and (b) tenth recursive prediction for Re = 590 are shown.
FIG. 5. Mean error per time-step for various models at Re = 590.
FIG. 6. (a) Comparison of fluctuating, periodic, and mean component error. (b) Estimated planar turbulent kinetic energy in model forecast and measurements across time-steps.
FIG. 8. (a) Comparison of fluctuating, periodic, and mean component error. (b) Estimated planar turbulent kinetic energy in model forecast and measurements across Reynolds number.

FIG. 9. Measured turbulence intensity in the cylinder wake at Re = 230, Re = 1180, and Re = 2920, as well as the corresponding mean error fields for turbulence intensity in surrogate model forecasts. The error is calculated as the difference between the actual and predicted turbulence intensity fields. Positive error values suggest models tend to underestimate turbulence intensity.

FIG. 10. Relationships between error, time-step, and Reynolds number for (a) U-Net, (b) FNO, (c) FCN, and (d) DMD models. On the left, the error is plotted as a function of prediction timestep across Reynolds number tested. On the right, the error at each timestep is plotted at individual time-steps.
FIG. 11. Relationship between error and time-step for models with and without a time-delay embedding.
